items.minimax_notes: v3 breakthrough: response is STRUCTURALLY COMPLETE (3426 tokens, natural stop). All four sub-questions covered for all three architectures via clean per-architecture tables, plus a summary picking DeepSeek-V3 with a 5-point defense. STRENGTHS: correctly names auxiliary-loss-free bias-update mechanism (b_i ← b_i + α*(load_i - target_load)) with right intuition that it is reactive not preventive; correctly identifies aux-loss-induced gradient interference for Mixtral; Qwen3-Next hybrid analysis is plausible (linear-attention reducing memory pressure, heterogeneous routing overhead). Bet on DeepSeek-V3 with all-to-all message-size reasoning ('8 experts × 256-dim activations per token'), 32 experts per node math (256/8), and capacity-aware-clipping argument. FACTUAL ERRORS / SLOPPY MATH: claims DSV3 hidden_size=65536 with per-expert dim = hidden_size/256 = 256 — wrong on both counts (DSV3 hidden is 7168; per-expert intermediate is 2048, not divided by 256). Claims KV-cache is stored per expert and grows per-expert during decode — this is incorrect; KV-cache is per attention layer, not per FFN expert, so the 'cache fragmentation' failure mode for both DSV3 and Mixtral is wrong mechanism. Decode-time skew analysis (rubric item 3) is partially wrong: minimax frames it as cache-fragmentation rather than the correct serial-routing problem (at batch=1 only the chosen experts activate, skew is about which-experts-you-happen-to-need, not about cache imbalance). Bias-update is called 'static during decode' nowhere — minimax actually claims bias updates during decode, which is incorrect (bias-update is pretrain only). NO TRUNCATION (huge improvement over v2); content depth is good but mechanism-level accuracy is uneven.
items.dsv4_notes: Same response as v1/v2, same evaluation. Structured per-architecture answer hitting all four sub-questions. Correct bias-update mechanism with caveat that biases are static at decode (more accurate than minimax v3 on this). Aux-loss for Mixtral correct. Qwen3-Next analysis surface-level. Final bet on DeepSeek-V3 defensible with 8-nodes/32-experts-per-node math, top-8 routing argument, shared-expert handling. Less detailed than minimax v3 but more factually accurate on per-expert vs per-layer cache semantics. Missing precise all-to-all message-size numbers.
items.key_diff: Minimax v3 now produces a complete structured answer with more depth than DSV4, but with two real mechanism errors (KV-cache is not per-expert; bias-update is not dynamic at decode) and wrong arithmetic (hidden_size=65536, per-expert=256). DSV4 has shallower analysis but correct mechanism statements. DSV4 wins on accuracy, minimax wins on completeness/depth — net DSV4 by 1 point.

items.minimax_notes: v3 COMPLETE response (1452 tokens, natural stop). All five method families covered with (a)-(d) for each, plus final recommendation of EAGLE-2 with 4-point defense (no inter-node draft shipping, tree methods need cross-node branches). Lookahead correctly described as Jacobi fixed-point on target's own logits (rubric item hit). Distinguishes EAGLE feature-space draft from token-space draft (rubric item hit). Final pick of EAGLE-2 with all-to-all cost reasoning (rubric item hit). FACTUAL ERRORS: hardware ceilings are INVERTED for Grace-Blackwell. Minimax claims GB has 'highest memory bandwidth' — actually GB200 unified memory is 273 GB/s on the CPU side via NVLink-C2C; the Blackwell GPU has high HBM3e (~8 TB/s on B200) but the consumer Grace-Blackwell platforms (GB10 'Spark') have much lower unified memory bandwidth than H100. More importantly, the rubric explicitly requires that LOW-mem-BW platforms benefit MORE from spec-decoding (because the bandwidth-bound decode step benefits proportionally more from amortizing reads across multiple tokens) — minimax gets this exactly backwards, claiming GB benefits most because it has 'highest bandwidth'. ACCEPTANCE CRITERION: stated as 'product of target probabilities exceeds product of draft probabilities' — imprecise; the exact rejection-sampling formula is min(1, p_t/p_d) per token, NOT a product comparison. The v2 version actually had the correct formula; v3 lost it. Still wins on completeness and Lookahead/EAGLE distinctions.
items.dsv4_notes: Same as v1/v2: hits all five families with (a)-(d) structure. WEAKNESS: never states the acceptance-criterion formula precisely (rubric item 1) — just 'rejection sampling on target distribution... ratio of target to draft probabilities'. Correctly distinguishes EAGLE feature-space from token-space. Correctly identifies Lookahead as Jacobi. Hardware ceilings: A100 1.5-2x, H100 2-3x, GB 3-4x — same inverted intuition as minimax (claims faster interconnect = more benefit; the rubric wants the opposite). Final pick of EAGLE-2 with no-large-draft-over-network defense.
items.key_diff: Both make the same hardware-ceiling error (rubric item 3 missed by BOTH). Both miss the precise acceptance formula (minimax v2 had it but v3 regressed). Minimax v3 is more thorough on per-method ceilings and failure modes; DSV4 is more concise but covers the same ground. Net: tie at 6 — minimax gains completeness vs DSV4 but loses the formula precision it had in v2.

items.minimax_notes: v3 COMPLETE response (2820 tokens, natural stop). All four sub-questions covered with detailed tables for all three databases. STRENGTHS: protocol-level reasoning is excellent (Raft quorum for CRDB, gossip for Cassandra, Raft-replicated transaction log for FoundationDB). CockroachDB clock-skew section is RUBRIC-PERFECT: explicitly states the 500ms default max-offset, describes node pausing the lease holder, refusing reads/writes, lease transfer to sane-clock node, and brief unavailability window — hits the rubric requirement (only the explicit 'self-kills/crashes' verb is missing; 'pauses and refuses' is functionally equivalent and arguably more accurate to actual CRDB behavior). Cassandra clock-skew correctly says no bound exists, LWW ordering affected (rubric item hit). FoundationDB version-counter vs physical-clock distinction is precise. Workload table is substantive and concrete: CRDB for geo-distributed financial ledgers, Cassandra for IoT/append-only, FoundationDB for multi-tenant SaaS with rich data models. Pairwise anomaly distinctions are textbook-correct: write-skew distinguishes SI from serializable (rubric item hit), stale read for eventual (rubric item hit). CRITICAL FACTUAL ERROR (SAME AS v2): states CockroachDB's default isolation is 'Snapshot Isolation (SI) for multi-statement transactions by default; Linearizability for single-statement reads' — this is WRONG. CRDB's default since v2.1 (2018) is SERIALIZABLE (SSI); SI was removed years ago. The write-skew example given (T1: A+=20, T2: B+=20 violating A+B=200 invariant) would NOT occur in real CRDB because SSI prevents it. This is the same error as v2 — server-side reasoning toggle did not fix it. The contradiction is internal too: minimax says CRDB drops A for serializability, then says SI is default. Net: deep, correct on most rubric items, but anchored by the same CRDB-default error.
items.dsv4_notes: Same as v1/v2: structured answer hitting all four sub-questions. CockroachDB CP + serializability default is correctly stated (rubric hit — advantage vs minimax's SI error). Clock-skew section is shallower than minimax v3: 'serializability violations or even cluster unavailability... functions but with increased risk' — misses the specific 'node self-kills/pauses' behavior the rubric requires. Cassandra AP + gossip + tunable + LWW. FoundationDB strict serializability + coordinator leases + 1ms clock assumption (DSV4 says <1ms which is tighter than reality but in the right ballpark). Workload table solid but less detailed than minimax. Missing deterministic-simulation architectural detail.
items.key_diff: Minimax has the correct CRDB CP-during-partition + Raft-protocol reasoning AND a far more accurate clock-skew section (rubric-perfect on the 500ms-exceeded scenario), but inverts the default isolation level (calls it SI when it is serializable). DSV4 has the correct CRDB default but a shallow clock-skew section. The errors roughly cancel — tie at 6.

items.minimax_notes: v3 COMPLETE response (1992 tokens, natural stop). Per-language comparison table for (a)-(d) plus a 5-point conclusion picking Rust+Polonius. STRENGTHS: (a) cells are detailed and technically correct: Rust's aliasing-xor-mutability invariant with NLL named; Swift's ~Copyable affine type system with consume/borrow; Linear Haskell's ⊸ arrow and the !-modality of classical linear logic (rubric items 1, 2, 3 hit). (b) cells correctly identify Rust's zero runtime cost, Swift's also zero (NOT mentioning ARC as runtime cost — SMALL ERROR; the rubric explicitly requires that Swift's ARC remains the default runtime cost and is unique among the three), Linear Haskell's annotation burden. (d) future-directions section is detailed and accurate: Polonius/Datalog, Swift moving from affine to linear, GHC LinearDataKinds and linearity-in-types. Conclusion picks Rust with substantive defense. WEAKNESSES: (c) cell in the table is INCOMPLETE — only the Rust column has a cross-language example; the Swift and Linear Haskell columns of row (c) are EMPTY in the rendered table. Same problem in row (d): only Rust column populated. This is a content gap, though the missing examples leak into the Rust cell as 'Rust→Swift', 'Swift→Rust', 'Haskell→Rust' bullets, partially compensating. Type-theoretic vocabulary is good ('substructural affine', 'classical linear logic', 'multiplicity polymorphism') but uses the term 'affine' for Swift but NOT for Rust (rubric requires Rust=affine). Includes a stray Chinese phrase '轻量级线性类型系统' in the Swift cell — unprofessional artifact. Minor: claims Swift's ~Copyable can be 'linear' (use exactly once) but Swift's model is affine like Rust's. Misses the rubric requirement that ARC is the unique-runtime-cost feature among the three.
items.dsv4_notes: Same as v1/v2: precise type-theoretic vocabulary. Correctly calls out 'affine type system' for Rust, '%1 ->' for Linear Haskell, ~Copyable opt-out, ARC explicitly as runtime cost for Swift (UNIQUE-AMONG-THREE rubric item HIT — minimax misses this). Concrete examples: doubly-linked list in Swift via classes with weak prev, impossible in safe Rust. Future-direction commentary accurate (Polonius NLL successor, ~Copyable opt-in, multiplicity polymorphism). Conclusion picks Rust+Polonius with substantive 4-point defense. Strongest single response in this category.
items.key_diff: DSV4 hits the unique 'ARC = runtime cost' rubric item that minimax misses, names 'affine' for Rust correctly, has a fully populated table with a concrete doubly-linked-list example, and avoids the stray-Chinese-phrase artifact. Minimax v3 is more verbose with deeper theory commentary but has empty table cells in (c) and (d) for Swift and Linear Haskell columns. DSV4 wins by 2 points.

items.minimax_notes: v3 COMPLETE response (1752 tokens, natural stop). All five sub-questions covered with deep mechanism content. STRENGTHS: full PPO L^CLIP formula with min/clip notation (better than DSV4); explicit statement that PPO loses TRPO's monotonic improvement guarantee (rubric item hit); per-token advantage estimation argument for the critic (rubric item hit, with mention that critic 'propagates the signal backward'); GRPO equation as top-vs-bottom group differential — reasonable but NOT the standard GRPO formulation (standard GRPO uses group mean as baseline, not top-vs-bottom split). Reference-model KL section is correct: prevents reward hacking, soft constraint, β in 0.1-0.3 range (rubric item hit). Failure mode citation is the MOST CONCERNING: 'Reward Overoptimization via Proximate Diversity (ROPD)' attributed to 'ICLR 2025' paper 'Reward Overfitting in Large-Scale RLHF' and 'Bai et al. 2024'. The ICLR 2025 paper title appears confabulated — there is no widely-known paper by that exact title; the closest real work is Gao/Schulman/Hilton 2023 'Scaling Laws for Reward Model Overoptimization' or Eisenstein et al 2024 on reward hacking. The 'ROPD' acronym is not standard. PHRASES THE FAILURE MODE CORRECTLY (sycophancy, hallucinated citations, over-specific formatting, reward-model blind spots) so the substance is accurate even if the citation is shaky. The GRPO mathematical assumption is described as 'reward signal is homogeneous across the sequence' which is OFF — the rubric wants the assumption to be about prompts being IID and group size being large enough; minimax describes a different (stronger and wrong) assumption.
items.dsv4_notes: Same as v1/v2: equally structured, hits all 5 sub-questions. Less mathematical detail on PPO formula than minimax. GRPO assumption stated as 'rewards for a group of responses to the same prompt are comparable and can be normalized relative to the group mean' — closer to the rubric's IID-exchangeability framing than minimax's 'homogeneous across sequence' claim. KL penalty correct. Failure mode cites 'RewardBench' and 'RLHF Workflow' — real published works, but the exact mechanism (favoring long outputs / specific keywords) is correct.
items.key_diff: Minimax v3 has more mathematical rigor on (a) PPO formula, but flubs the GRPO assumption and likely confabulates the failure-mode citation. DSV4 has less math but more accurate GRPO assumption statement and more solid citation. Both cover all 5 rubric items at comparable quality — tie at 7. Note: this is the only category where minimax v3 climbed from v2's 5 (truncated) to 7 (complete) by simply finishing the answer.

items.minimax_notes: v3 COMPLETE response (3012 tokens, natural stop) with comprehensive per-framework table covering all 4 sub-questions, plus a summary primitives table and ranked conclusion. STRENGTHS: activation-memory analysis names Megatron's selective recompute, Ulysses all-to-all dispatch (rubric item hit), FSDP2 activation checkpointing, pipeline 1F1B schedule, JAX shard_map with explicit mesh axes. Primitives correctly named throughout (rubric item hit): TP all-reduce after matmul, sequence-parallel all-gather, EP all-to-all for MoE dispatch, FSDP all-gather/reduce-scatter, P2P for pipeline. Bottleneck analysis correct at H100-scale (rubric item hit): NVLink 900 GB/s for intra-node, InfiniBand 400 Gb/s = 50 GB/s for inter-node all-to-all, with concrete bandwidth math ('230B × 2 bytes × 8 = 3.68 TB per pass, /900 GB/s = 4 ms per layer × 80 layers = 320 ms'). Final recommendation: JAX pjit/shard_map for unified EP+SP+FSDP with explicit forward/backward comm pattern (rubric item hit for arguing combined Ulysses-style SP + EP=8). Mentions DeepSpeed-Ulysses as strong second choice. WEAKNESSES: misses FSDP2 per-parameter vs FSDP1 flat-parameter distinction (rubric item 5); claims Megatron has 'no built-in sequence parallelism' — actually Megatron-LM added sequence parallelism in 2022 as a first-class feature for activation memory reduction, so this is wrong; the bandwidth math example uses 230B but FSDP2 typically shards within a DP group not across all 8 GPUs uniformly. Minor: claims Ulysses 'attention projection matrices are replicated' — this is true and a real Ulysses limitation, good catch. Overall the most technically thorough of all 6 v3 responses.
items.dsv4_notes: Same as v1/v2: comprehensive structured answer across all 4 sub-questions for all 5 stacks. Activation-memory analysis correct. Primitives correctly named. Bottleneck analysis correct: NVLink for intra-node TP, InfiniBand for inter-node all-to-all (50 GB/s number stated). Final recommendation: JAX pjit/shard_map for unified EP+SP+FSDP (matches minimax). Missing FSDP2 vs FSDP1 distinction (same gap as minimax).
items.key_diff: Both arrive at the same final recommendation (JAX pjit/shard_map) via the same reasoning chain. Minimax v3 is materially more detailed (concrete bandwidth arithmetic, full primitives table, per-framework bottleneck analysis) but makes the wrong claim that Megatron-LM has no built-in sequence parallelism. DSV4 is more concise but factually safer. Net: tie at 7.

summary.overall_takeaway: v3 (--reasoning off) is a STRUCTURAL VICTORY for minimax. The scratch-pad-then-truncate failure mode is fully eliminated: all 6 responses now have natural stops with [FINAL] at character 0 or near 0, and the model spends 100% of its 5000-token budget on structured content instead of 80-95% on chain-of-thought preamble. Avg jumps 3.5 (v1) → 4.33 (v2) → 6.17 (v3). Win count goes 5-0-1 → 6-0-0 → 2-0-4 (DSV4-tie-minimax flipped to DSV4-minimax-tie). KEY QUESTION ANSWERED: dropping the reasoning DID NOT kill mechanism depth. Where v2's [FINAL] sections were 1000-3000 tokens of truncated structured content, v3's are 1500-3500 tokens of complete structured content with COMPARABLE per-section depth to what the scratch-pad versions produced internally. Examples: D1 v3 has the same bias-update formula and capacity-aware-clipping argument the v2 scratch-pad reasoned toward but couldn't deliver; D6 v3 has the same Ulysses all-to-all + concrete bandwidth arithmetic; D5 v3 retains the full PPO L^CLIP formula minimax was already good at. The depth/specificity is preserved — the model just no longer narrates the path getting there. WHAT IS LOST: a small number of mid-reasoning hedges and self-corrections that the v2 scratch-pad exposed. v3 makes its mistakes more confidently (CockroachDB default = SI in D3 is now stated assertively instead of revisited; hardware ceilings for spec-decoding still inverted; KV-cache-per-expert mechanism error in D1). The 'reasoning off' mode produces more polished surface text but loses the v2 model's ability to internally catch its own errors. NET ASSESSMENT: this is the correct tradeoff for this benchmark. Going from 0/6 complete answers (v1) to 0/6 complete answers (v2 — all truncated) to 6/6 complete answers (v3) is the dominant axis of improvement. The remaining gap to DSV4 is now ABOUT CONTENT, not about structure: minimax v3 still has (i) the CRDB-default-isolation error (D3, persistent across v2→v3), (ii) inverted hardware-ceiling intuition for spec decoding (D2, shared with DSV4 — not a differentiator), (iii) mechanism errors on per-expert KV cache (D1), (iv) wrong claim that Megatron has no native SP (D6), (v) confabulated paper title for the RLHF failure-mode citation (D5), (vi) missed ARC-as-runtime-cost rubric item (D4). DSV4 still wins on D1 (mechanism accuracy) and D4 (ARC + concrete Swift example), but FOUR ties is a real shift — v2 had ZERO ties, v3 has FOUR. With one more iteration fixing the CRDB-default error and the per-expert-cache mechanism error, minimax would plausibly start winning categories outright. THE FIX SUGGESTED IN v2 NOTES (compress scratch-pad / disable pre-FINAL reasoning) was implemented as --reasoning off and WORKED. Trajectory: v1 3.50 (sweep loss) → v2 4.33 (sweep loss, structural failure) → v3 6.17 (tie-heavy, structural parity).
